intro:
Timothy (Greek Timotheos, “honouring God”) was the apostle Paul’s closest and most trusted co-worker. A native of Lystra in Lycaonia, he was of mixed heritage — son of a Jewish Christian mother and a Greek father (Acts 16:1). From childhood he had been nurtured in the Scriptures by his mother Eunice and grandmother Lois, whose “sincere faith” Paul commended as now dwelling in Timothy (2 Timothy 1:5; 2 Timothy 3:15).

On his second missionary journey Paul recruited the young disciple, well spoken of by the brethren, and circumcised him so as not to needlessly offend the Jews they would meet (Acts 16:2-3). Timothy became Paul’s constant companion through Macedonia, Achaia, and Asia, and his trusted emissary to churches in crisis — sent to strengthen the Thessalonians (1 Thessalonians 3:2), to remind the Corinthians of Paul’s ways (1 Corinthians 4:17), and to bring news from Philippi, where Paul testified, “I have no one like him… as a son with a father he has served with me in the gospel” (Philippians 2:19-22). He is named as co-sender of several of Paul’s letters (2 Corinthians 1:1; Colossians 1:1).

Paul addressed him most tenderly — “to Timothy, my true child in the faith” (1 Timothy 1:2) — yet also bracingly, for Timothy seems to have been of a timid and sometimes ailing disposition (1 Timothy 5:23; 2 Timothy 1:7). Paul left him in Ephesus to guard the church against false teaching (1 Timothy 1:3), and the two Pastoral Epistles bearing his name urge him to “fight the good fight of faith,” guard the deposit entrusted to him, and not let others despise his youth but set an example in word and conduct (1 Timothy 4:12; 1 Timothy 6:12).

In Paul’s final letter, written as he awaited execution, the apostle longs to see his son once more and charges him with his great commission: “preach the word… in season and out of season… do the work of an evangelist, fulfill your ministry” (2 Timothy 4:1-5). Hebrews notes Timothy’s own release from imprisonment (Hebrews 13:23). Timothy stands as the New Testament’s model of the faithful younger minister — formed by the Scriptures, mentored by an apostle, and entrusted with guarding and passing on sound doctrine to the next generation (2 Timothy 2:2).
